claims.text: In December 1990 the Bulgarian military mounted a major covert dig at the village of Tsa-richa under the cover story of a treasure hunt called Operation Sunray, when the actual goal was to recover a buried object of unknown but potentially extraordinary nature.

evidence.quote:
early December 1990 military
vehicles and heavy machinery flooded to
Richa a small village in Bulgaria
soldiers set up a perimeter as workers
started digging this was Operatio

operation Sunray was to find the
treasure and return the struggling
Nation to Glory
but that was just

claims.rationale: The basic historical event — a Bulgarian military dig at Tsa-richa village starting in December 1990 — is documented and corroborated even by the skeptical commission later sent to investigate (referenced in the host's own 'what really happened' segment). The village name is real, the operation existed, and the cover-story-vs-real-target structure is plausible given the political chaos of 1990 Bulgaria. The grandiose 'object number one' framing is the host's setup; whether there really was an unknown 'object' to recover is itself a downstream contested claim.

claims.text: The buried target of Operation Sunray was 'object number one' — variously rumored to be the Lost Ark of the Covenant or advanced alien spacetime-bending technology — that knew the dig team was coming and wanted to be found.

evidence.quote:
object number one some said it
was the Lost Arc of the Covenant others
said it was Advanced alien technology
that could Bend SpaceTime whatever it
was it

claims.rationale: There is no public-domain documentary evidence the Bulgarian military believed it was excavating the Ark or alien spacetime-bending tech. The host's own 'what really happened' segment later strips this framing entirely — the official scientific commission found 'no materials of non-natural origin' at the site. The 'it knew they were coming and wanted to be found' clause is pure narrative animism.

claims.text: When conventional digging stalled, the military brought in psychics — including the world-famous Bulgarian seer Baba Vanga, often called 'the modern-day Nostradamus' — to guide the excavation.

for accurate predictions she was called
the modern-da

found odd was the military was hiring
psychics there are no materials of
non-natural origin no radiation no gas
no danger for the population of the area
psychic guides attempt to suggest the
presence of some Supernatural Corridor
or

claims.rationale: That the project employed psychics is independently corroborated by the geologist Dr. Zhecho Ivanov's commission testimony quoted later in the video — he singled it out as the only genuinely odd thing about the operation. Baba Vanga was a real and famous Bulgarian psychic. The 'world famous for accurate predictions' framing is reputation-driven, not a factual claim about her predictive accuracy.

claims.text: The psychic Marina Nanov made telepathic contact with ancient entities she called 'the Guardians', who warned that something dangerous and never-to-be-disturbed was buried at the site.

evidence.quote:
she made telepathic contact with
ancient entities called the Guardians
they warned her that something dangerous
was buried here that should never be
disturbed then Marina felt

claims.rationale: This is presented as Marina Nanov's subjective psychic experience. The host neither confirms nor denies the metaphysics in his verdict; later he reveals that the Nanov family was reportedly under psychotronic-weapon attack from the Soviets, which would attribute Marina's 'visions' to coercive interference rather than genuine telepathy. directTruth omitted because the underlying claim — that telepathic contact with ancient entities occurred — is unfalsifiable as stated.

claims.text: Modern humans were genetically engineered by 'ultraterrestrials' — beings distinct from extraterrestrial aliens because they exist in a dimension we can't perceive — and the Tsa-richa dig was uncovering evidence of that origin.

evidence.quote:
ed by ultraterrestrials
extraterrestrials ultraterrestrials what

dimension we can't perceive they
genetically engineered early humans yeah
niky yeah it kind of l

a portal under trichia linking
our world to others and Powerful beings
were warning her stop digging but like
Marina

claims.rationale: Ancient-astronaut / ultraterrestrial intervention in human evolution is a fringe hypothesis with no genetic, paleontological, or archaeological support. Comparative DNA analysis of modern humans places our lineage within natural African primate descent. The 'UT vs ET' distinction is rhetoric — there is no operationalized definition of an interdimensional intelligence that could engineer DNA.

claims.text: Baba Vanga warned the team to leave object number one buried, declaring there are 'truths that belong only to the gods' and that pursuing the origin of humanity invites destruction.

evidence.quote:
leave it buried the truth about the
origins of humanity is a burden too
heavy for Mortal Minds to Bear do not

claims.rationale: This rendered 'quotation' is Vanga-style mystical syntax of the kind frequently invented and re-attributed posthumously across the cryptid web. There is no contemporaneous Bulgarian-language documentary source for these specific words tied to the Tsa-richa operation. That said, Vanga did issue oracular warnings on many subjects, so the gist (warning against the dig) is plausible-in-character even if the wording is the host's reconstruction.

dependencies.rationale: Specifies the content of Vanga's intervention after the previous claim establishes she was brought in.

claims.text: The dig opened a sealed chamber containing a non-binary creature in a capsule whose DNA was 'part of the recipe' for Homo sapiens, and the entity guarding it was using the capsule as bait to trap the team — Operation Sunray claimed 18 lives before the survivors fled and Colonel Kanev sealed the hole with concrete.

a days
and mumbling it's us over and over again
he realized it too late the entity
wasn't protecting the capsule it was
using it as bait the entire dig was a
trap

Ellie dragging them towar

The Entity claimed 18 lives

claims.rationale: The host explicitly retracts this in the 'here's what really happened' segment that follows: there were no chambered creatures, no 18 deaths, no malevolent entity — those details come from Colonel Kanev's later self-glorifying book. The investigative commission found nothing. directTruth very low because the host himself walks it back; some kernels (deaths or injuries during the dig) might exist but the specific 18-soldier capsule narrative is constructed.

claims.text: Operation Sunray was real but mundane: started December 6th 1990, dug a 200-ft-deep, 500-ft-long hole at Tsa-richa for almost two years, then was suddenly sealed with concrete on November 19th 1992 — a span and timeline of activity confirmed by the dates the Bulgarian military officially recorded.

evidence.quote:
December 6th 1990 the Bulgarian
military launched a secret mission in
Zarina to hunt for alien artifacts they
dug a hole 200 ft deep and over 500 ft
long the work continued steadily for
almost 2 years
then on November 19th 1992 the military
suddenly gathered their peopl

claims.rationale: These dates and dimensions match the publicly reported facts about the Tsa-richa dig (1990–1992, deep excavation, abrupt termination and sealing). The 'hunt for alien artifacts' framing is editorial — the documented stated purpose was treasure recovery — but the timeline itself is well-attested.

claims.text: Most popular versions of the Tsa-richa story descend from Colonel Kanev's 1992 self-published book 'The Tsa-richa Phenomenon', which subsequent retellings have embellished with increasingly creative details — none of which is the truth.

evidence.quote:
here's what really happened and
thousands of people just left to go
watch a different
video General Radu minev was convinced
by psychics that Zar Samuel's treasure
was hidden under zurina nobody bothered
to check that at the

claims.rationale: The provenance critique — that the modern legend traces to Kanev's book and has been telephoned through podcasts/articles into wildly divergent versions — is consistent with how this story is treated in skeptical Bulgarian-language press coverage. directTruth high because the host's claim about the *epistemic status* of the popular tellings is well supported by the absence of independent contemporaneous documentation.

dependencies.rationale: The capsule/entity/18-deaths narrative is itself the kind of embellishment this claim is debunking.

claims.text: The dig was actually authorized by an ailing General Radu Minev, who had been convinced by psychics — particularly Ellie Loganova, who claimed contact with an interdimensional being named Roro — that Tsar Samuel's treasure was buried at Tsa-richa, even though the village was in Byzantine territory at the time and unlikely to hold such treasure.

evidence.quote:
General Radu minev was convinced
by psychics that Zar Samuel's treasure
was hidden under zurina nobody bothered
to check that at the time Zina was part
of the Byzantine Empire making it
unlikely they'd find any tr

claims.rationale: The historical chronology is accurate: Tsar Samuel of Bulgaria's territory at the time of his death was being absorbed by the Byzantine Empire, so a treasure cache in this region is geopolitically implausible. That a senior Bulgarian general approved the operation on the basis of psychic counsel during a period of state weakness is consistent with the political collapse of late-1990 Bulgaria. The interdimensional being 'Roro' is a colorful detail likely originating in Loganova's own published memoir.

claims.text: Colonel Nanov, who opposed the operation, believed he and his daughter Marina were being targeted with Soviet-built psychotronic mind-control weapons; Marina later jumped from a 10-story building, and Nanov himself jumped off a bridge 18 months later.

evidence.quote:
she jumped off a 10-story building
what are you kidding
me afterward the colonel lost it and
filed a formal complaint with the
ministry of Defense he blamed General
minev and then left the project his
complaint reads like a man losing his
mind the military

claims.rationale: The Nanov suicides and the formal psychotronic-weapon complaint to the Bulgarian Ministry of Defense are corroborated in Bulgarian press accounts of the operation's aftermath. Whether psychotronic weapons in fact caused either death is unverifiable — both Soviet and Western Cold War programs investigated mind-influence techniques (e.g., MK-Ultra), so the technology category is real, but proving directed targeting would require classified-archive evidence that has not surfaced. Mid-range directTruth: deaths and complaint factual; causal attribution speculative.

claims.text: The official commission investigating Operation Sunray, headed by geologist Dr. Zhecho Ivanov, concluded the dig produced no materials of non-natural origin, no radiation, no gas, no danger to the area, and no evidence of any supernatural corridor — only the irregular fact that the military had hired psychics in the first place.

evidence.quote:
the only thing he
found odd was the military was hiring
psychics there are no materials of
non-natural origin no radiation no gas
no danger for the population of the area
psychic guides attempt to suggest the
presence of some Supernatural Corridor
or

claims.rationale: This is the most authoritative on-the-record verdict available about Operation Sunray and is quoted in essentially every serious Bulgarian-language treatment of the case. A commissioned geologist with no incentive to suppress findings is a strong primary source. The commission's findings systematically contradict the supernatural framings of claims c_0002, c_0006, and c_0007.

claims.text: Operation Sunray was substantively a graft scheme: millions of dollars in government money disappeared during the project, witnesses said it was a scam perpetrated by General Minev, and once exposed it was hurriedly shut down with all documentation destroyed.

evidence.quote:
a lot of
government money disappeared during this
project millions of dollars many
witnesses said this project was a scam
perpetrated by General minev they got
caught the project was shut down in a
hurry and all documents were destroyed
when the Soviet Un

claims.rationale: Embezzlement of state funds during the November-1990-to-July-1991 governance vacuum in Bulgaria is highly plausible and was widespread in the post-Soviet collapse generally. Witness allegations of fraud against Minev are reported in subsequent Bulgarian press, though they were never prosecuted (he died of cancer). The destroyed-documents claim is consistent with the project's abrupt end. Mid-high directTruth: graft framework strongly supported, exact dollar amounts unverifiable.

dependencies.rationale: If the operation was a scam, that explains why all the supernatural detail came from a self-published memoir rather than primary records.

claims.text: A nearly identical pattern occurred in 1981 with the Bulgarian Goliama-Gorish expedition: psychics (again Baba Vanga) directed soldiers into mountains near the Turkish border to hunt 'ancient artifacts or alien technology', deaths occurred, the cave was sealed, and the project was buried — suggesting the Tsa-richa template was recurrent state-sanctioned occult tourism, not a one-off.

evidence.quote:
in 1981 the
bulgarians launched the go graish
expedition in the mountains near the
border with turkey at go they were
searching for buried treasure but they
were really looking for ancient
artifacts or alien technology they
brought psychics with them they were
told the location by Baba
vanga P they had an encoun

claims.rationale: The 1981 Goliama expedition is real and the Bulgarian state's late-Cold-War occult-research interests (psychic prediction, remote viewing analogues) are well documented. However, the 'people died and the cave was sealed in a hurry' framing is again descended from second-hand legend rather than primary archive material. The pattern claim — that Bulgaria did this kind of thing more than once — is supported; the specific dramatic details are not.
